channel.title: Prism
channel.description: Science, nature, and the questions we're still answering

item.title: The Trees That Talk to Each Other Underground
item.description:
Beneath the forest floor, a web of fungal threads connects the roots of trees across hundreds of metres. Through this network, trees share nutrients, send chemical warnings about insect attacks, and even subsidise younger saplings growing in the shade.

Ecologist Suzanne Simard first described this "wood wide web" in the late 1990s. Since then, researchers have mapped mycorrhizal networks in forests on every continent except Antarctica, revealing a level of cooperation that challenges the Darwinian image of nature as purely competitive.

The oldest trees, the ones Simard calls "mother trees," function as hubs, connected to hundreds of neighbours. When they fall, the network doesn't collapse; it reorganises. The forest, it turns out, has been doing distributed computing far longer than we have.

item.title: What Octopuses Dream About
item.description:
When an octopus sleeps, its skin ripples through a kaleidoscope of colours and textures. Researchers at the University of Washington believe these displays correspond to dream states, much like the rapid eye movements in sleeping mammals.

The colours shift in patterns that mirror waking behaviour: hunting sequences, camouflage responses, even what appears to be social signalling. If the octopus is dreaming, it may be replaying its day in chromatophore.

What makes this remarkable is that octopus brains are organised nothing like ours. Two-thirds of their neurons are in their arms, not their heads. If they dream, they've arrived at the same cognitive trick through an entirely different architecture, suggesting that sleep and dreaming may be more fundamental to intelligence than we assumed.

item.title: The Deepest Hole Ever Drilled (and Why We Stopped)
item.description:
In 1970, Soviet scientists began drilling into the Kola Peninsula with a simple goal: go as deep as possible. Over the next 24 years, they reached 12,262 metres, a record that still stands. Then they stopped, not because they ran out of ambition, but because the Earth fought back.

At that depth, temperatures hit 180 degrees Celsius, far hotter than models predicted. The rock behaved less like a solid and more like a plastic, closing the borehole behind the drill bit. The project revealed that our understanding of what lies beneath the crust was, in many ways, guesswork.

We've sent probes to the edge of the solar system, but the deepest we've ever gone into our own planet is about 0.2 percent of the way to the centre. The Earth keeps most of its secrets close.

item.title: Why the Night Sky Is Disappearing
item.description:
A third of humanity can no longer see the Milky Way. Light pollution is increasing at roughly 10 percent per year, driven by LED lighting that is cheap, efficient, and pointed in every direction except useful ones.

The ecological consequences are now well-documented. Migratory birds lose their bearings. Sea turtle hatchlings crawl toward streetlights instead of the ocean. Insects, which navigate by moonlight, spiral into bulbs until they die of exhaustion.

But there's a human cost too. For most of history, every person who looked up at night saw the same staggering canopy of stars. That shared experience is quietly vanishing, replaced by an orange glow that tells us nothing about where we are in the universe.

item.title: The Glacier That Remembers Everything
item.description:
Ice cores drilled from glaciers in Greenland and Antarctica contain air bubbles trapped hundreds of thousands of years ago. Each bubble is a tiny time capsule, preserving the exact composition of the atmosphere on the day the snow fell.

By reading these bubbles layer by layer, scientists have reconstructed 800,000 years of climate history with startling precision. They can identify individual volcanic eruptions, trace the rise of agriculture through methane spikes, and watch carbon dioxide levels climb and fall with ice ages.

The current CO2 concentration, around 425 parts per million, is higher than anything in that 800,000-year record. The ice doesn't editorialize. It just reports. But the data speaks clearly enough.

item.title: The Fungus That Eats Radiation for Breakfast
item.description:
Inside the ruined reactor at Chernobyl, a black fungus grows toward the radiation source the way a plant grows toward light. Cladosporium sphaerospermum doesn't just tolerate ionising radiation — it appears to use it as an energy source, converting gamma rays into chemical energy through a process called radiosynthesis.

The melanin in its cell walls absorbs radiation and drives metabolic reactions, much the way chlorophyll absorbs sunlight. It's photosynthesis's dark twin, powered not by a star but by a melted nuclear core.

NASA is now studying the fungus as a potential radiation shield for spacecraft. A thin layer of living fungus could, in theory, protect astronauts on long-duration missions — a biological solution to a problem we've been trying to solve with lead and concrete.

item.title: Why Crows Remember Your Face
item.description:
Researchers at the University of Washington wore masks while trapping and banding crows on campus. Years later, crows still mobbed anyone wearing those specific masks — even though the original birds' offspring, who had never been trapped, joined in.

Crows don't just recognise individual human faces. They teach their young which faces are dangerous. The information propagates through the social group like a warning passed down through generations, creating a cultural memory that outlasts any single bird's lifespan.

Brain scans show that when a crow sees a threatening face, the same neural pathways light up as in a human experiencing fear and recognition. They aren't just reacting — they're remembering, evaluating, and deciding. The line between instinct and cognition blurs more with every study.
